content.boundary: The Polish 1999 case shows a structural reform pattern and closure side-effect, but the achievement-gap benefit is contested in the literature, and no Hungarian pilot data exist to confirm transferability.
content.change_lever: Poland's 1999 educational reform (reducing primary school from 8 to 6 years and creating larger-catchment lower-secondary schools) was explicitly designed to improve rural education quality but inadvertently reduced primary school size and triggered rural school closures as a structural side effect.; Delayed secondary tracking is associated in some international comparative literature with reduced achievement gaps, though the strength and causal attribution of this association (versus other confounding system features) is disputed among comparative-education researchers.
content.name: Structural grade reorganization (lower grades stay, upper grades centralize)
content.system_problem: Restructure the school system so only lower grades (1-4/1-6) remain in the village while upper grades or a new ISCED2-type school are consolidated in a larger town, following the Polish 1999 structural-reform pattern of shortened primary plus centrally sited later-stage schooling.
